raw_payload.author: zkonedog
raw_payload.content:
After the first 20-or-so minutes of director Maggie Gyllenhaal's The Bride, I was hooked. An utterly entrancing beginning sets up what seems to portend a whip-smart, feminist-centric new take on the Frankenstein story. But by the time the credits rolled, I found myself utterly overwhelmed and quite lost as to what I had just watched.

For a very basic overview, The Bride tells the story of the titular character, Ida (Jessie Buckley), a dead woman re-animated by Dr. Euphronius (Annette Bening) as a companion for the lonely Frank (Christian Bale). As the newfound pair galivant off for the first time, they are pursued by detectives Myrna (Penelope Cruz) & Jake (Peter Sarsgaard) who are mystified as to how Ida is still upright.

The setup to The Bride is truly intriguing. The idea of playing with Mary Shelley's authorship and self as being interspersed with the Bride character is a strong one, especially as personified by Buckley's tremendous acting talent. Bale as a long-suffering Frankenstein's monster is both humorous and affecting, while Bening as the mad-scientist in charge of re-animation steals some scenes in the early goings. Dialogue and sight gags absolutely crackle with energy and I found myself really excited for the rest of the film.

Sadly, as soon as Frank & Ida are released upon the world, everything in The Bride comes unglued. The issue here is that Gyllenhaal's manic, caterwauling style leaves not an ounce of room for explanation or contemplation. In essence: the idea is fun & fresh, but as soon as any mechanics come into play viewers are going to be extremely confused plot-wise. What influence does the Shelley persona actually have? Why are the detectives pursuing Ida, again? Why is Frank obsessed with the song-and-dance films of Ronnie Reed (Jake Gyllenhaal)? All these concepts (and many more) are left nebulous in favor of frantic cinematography and general visual/dialogue chaos.

Worse yet: even the strong feminist take on the subject matter so expertly teased in the opening act is overshadowed by the over-stimulating ludicrousness of everything else. Just a touch of restraint in characters/messaging would have gone a long way from Gyllenhaal in this flick. Alas, The Bride gives no quarter to nuance whatsoever. Instead of a conversation, this film is a wild scream into the abyss.

I suppose a more charitable interpretation of The Bride would be something akin to "the female voice as a wild scream of frustration and freedom". But in order for that approach to move viewers, it requires complete immersion in the wild propulsive energy of the piece and utter disregard for plot or character-development outside of their basic elements.

Overall, I give The Bride a disappointing 4/10 star ranking. I'll give it some credit for an interesting idea and an immediately-compelling setup, but it mostly squanders all such goodwill by showing no restraint or nuance other than noise, audacity, and broad strokes.

raw_payload.author: FeastMode

Weirdness in movies is completely subjective. Sometimes I find a movie so weird and I love it. It's my kind of weird. The weirdness makes it memorable (examples, The Substance and Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die).

Other times I strongly dislike the weirdness, especially if it's seemingly there for no reason. It feels pretentious (Die My Love, Poor things). The Bride! Has both kinds. So it was a mixed bag for me.

The performances from both leads are amazing. It's great to see the GOAT actor Christian Bale back on screen. He plays the role so well. I never knew I needed Christian Bale-enstein in my life. And the musical score in this movie is fantastic. In some parts, like the opening title card, it's epic.

Things that brought it down for me include the song and dance numbers. It made it feel like a musical. And the inclusion of Mary Shelley as a character feels strange and unnecessary. I also couldn't understand large portions of the dialogue, due to some combination of strong accents and sound mixing.

I didn't mind seeing this, especially in IMAX with the loud speakers and expanded aspect ratio. I just wish I liked it more.

(1 viewing, opening Thursday IMAX 3/5/2026)

raw_payload.author: movieman6-413-929510
raw_payload.content:
Set in 1930, the story follows Frankenstein's monster, now living under the name Frank (Christian Bale). Despite his new identity, he remains deeply lonely and asks Dr. Euphronius (Annette Bening) to help him bring a deceased woman back to life. The result is The Bride (Jessie Buckley), who awakens not only with a new body but also seemingly with the spirit of Mary Shelley inside her, causing two personalities to alternate control.

While Frank hopes for companionship, The Bride wants something different: to explore the world and discover who she really is. Their journey quickly spirals into chaos as they attract the attention of the police and unintentionally inspire radical social movements.

Gyllenhaal attempts to combine several contemporary themes, including modern loneliness and feminist ideas connected to the MeToo movement. In theory, the concept of a woman created to satisfy a lonely man questioning her own identity could have been compelling. In practice, however, the film feels overcrowded with ideas.

The central premise of The Bride discovering her identity would already have been strong enough. Adding Mary Shelley's spirit only complicates things further. Jessie Buckley convincingly plays the shifting personalities, at times almost resembling a female version of Gollum, but the internal conflict rarely becomes truly engaging.

Christian Bale and Buckley try to carry the film, yet the unfocused script holds them back. With a tighter narrative and fewer subplots, The Bride might have been a much stronger and clearer film.

raw_payload.author: davidhock

About halfway through The Bride!, I found myself doing the one thing no director wants: checking my watch. I went in wanting to love it, but the experience was less like a cohesive film and more like a cluttered attic.

The issue is the sheer lack of focus. It's a chaotic pile-up of genres, 1930s noir, punk-rock riot, and Mary Shelley possession stories all fighting for oxygen. Instead of the parts being stitched together to create a new life, the stitches are all you see.

The layering simply doesn't connect. The "Mary Shelley" voiceovers felt like a separate track, and the "Bonnie and Clyde" road trip felt like it was from a different movie entirely. Christian Bale and Jessie Buckley are giving it everything, but they're trapped in a monstrosity of half-finished flourishes. When the heavy-handed monologues started clashing with the erratic stylization, the film didn't just feel messy, it felt noisy.

It has the parts, but it never finds the rhythm. If you find yourself looking at the time at the 60-minute mark, it's not you it's the movie.

raw_payload.author: ZlatanSkorsezi

The Bride! Is a terribly slow, misguided, incoherent mess. It plays like a huge swing at something bold and operatic, but instead collapses under its own weight. The pacing is glacial, the script is atrociously written, and the whole film drifts between tones without any real control. Scenes stretch endlessly, and the story never finds a clear rhythm or direction.

What makes it worse is that the film constantly insists on its own importance. Big visual gestures and theatrical performances try to sell the illusion of something profound, but underneath it all there's surprisingly little substance. It feels less like a daring reimagining and more like an indulgent experiment that no one involved quite knew how to shape.

Jessie Buckley dropping her own Catwoman a week before she probably wins an Oscar is quite a career move. An incoherent, terribly paced, atrociously written big swing that ends up an embarrassment for everyone involved.

raw_payload.author: dorMancyx

I thought it's about the incompatibility of star-crossed lovers but it's really a genuine romance; I thought it's about the unseen goods in "monsters" but then they take hostages and kill innocents; I thought it's a play on trends and protofeminism but then Penelope Cruz's character pulls out another of those cliche "Oh the detective? You're looking at her" moves toward the end. That's not it of the writer's ambition, as she jams multiple completely superfluous plot lines into a movie that should just be about "The Bride," like Mary Shelley's apparition, the mobs' involvement, the guy detective's obsession, the mad doctor's backstory, Jake Gyllenhaal's cameo as the movie star... God save me from the confusion and boredom in the last hour. Performances are about what you'd expect of Buckley and Bale, plus I think the overall theatrical and physical methods of display are beneath their full competence. Music, cinematography, visuals are plainly unmemorable. Costume, makeup, and some of the mise-en-scene are decent. In all, I would prefer not to watch.

This movie aspires to cult film status. Maybe in twenty years Maggie Gyllenhall's effort here will be accorded such status, if only for its collection of celebrated stars being bizarre fingerpainted caricatures of movie archetypes. But I doubt it.

I'm not sure why rhis film was made or who this movie was made for, other than the players, who all serm to be having lots of fun. I do expect this: The Bride! Will be a big winner at the next Razzie Awards.

raw_payload.author: waltermwilliams
raw_payload.content:
The directorial debut of actress Maggie Gyllenhaal "Lost Daughter" was one of my least favourite movies of 2021...fast forward to 2026 and one the most anticipated films of the year is her second project "The Bride" that she also wrote.

It opens with a soliloquy from long dead author of "Frankenstein" Mary Shelley who then possesses the body of a key witness "Ida" who then dies and is reanimated as "The Bride".

All three characters being deftly played by Jessie Buckley.

She is the stand out in this film, even overshadowing the ever amazing Christian Bale.

The film takes inspiration from the 1935 film "Bride of Frankenstein", which was inspired by creator Mary Shelley's 1818 novel.

This story is set in 1935 Chicago, with Dr. Frankenstein's Monster (Bale) enlisting the help of Dr. Euphronius (Annette Benning) to create a companion for him.

The film contains some visual homages to Fritz Lang's Metropolis (1927) and also a dance sequence that felt like an homage to Mel Brooks 1974 parody "Young Frankenstein" complete with the song, "Puttin' on the Ritz".

"The Bride" features two Academy Award winners: Bale and Penelope Cruz; and three Academy Award nominees: Buckley, Jake Gyllenhaal and Benning

By the third act the loved up couple of Monsters turn into "Bonnie & Clyde" on a crime spree across America.

Meanwhile "The Bride" has involuntarily started her own feminist movement.

"The Bride" has as many split personalities and tourette's as its heroine protagonist, which muddies the waters of what could have been an interesting story and film.

Instead it becomes messy and bloated with over 2 hours of screen time.

When the curtain goes down on this oddly weird, indie style movie "The Bride" , and you think it couldn't get any stranger, the incongruous classic comedy Halloween song "The Monster Mash" by Bobby "Boris" Pickett plays out the credits.

It feels like Baz Luhrmann's dance troops had an Arkham Asylum storyline.

Here comes the mother 'ing Bride with more style over substance!

For my money give me the OG "Bride of Frankenstein" (1935) rather than this messy montage.

raw_payload.author: kevin_robbins
raw_payload.content:
My daughter and I watched The Bride (2026) in theaters. The storyline follows the Monster in the early 1900s, now residing in Chicago. More than 100 years after the death of his creator, Frankenstein, the Monster longs for the one thing he's never had-a companion. He finds a doctor who can help him, but the woman revived to be his companion awakens with amnesia and what seems like a split personality. As she begins to piece together who she is, a strange love story unfolds.

This picture is written and directed by Maggie Gyllenhaal (The Lost Daughter) and stars Jessie Buckley (Hamnet), Christian Bale (The Dark Knight), Jake Gyllenhaal (Nightcrawler), Penélope Cruz (Vanilla Sky), and Peter Sarsgaard (Garden State).

The Bride is a bit of a mixed bag, with some fun scenes but uneven writing and execution. The storyline had potential, blending horror, crime drama, and character study while also trying to lean into a noir style. Unfortunately, most of those elements didn't quite work for me. The film is well shot, and I enjoyed the depiction of the era, while Christian Bale was excellent in portraying the Monster's outlook and personality.

However, I'm not sure Jessie Buckley worked as the Bride for me. Some of that may be due to the writing. I didn't love the way her character spoke or what she was given to deliver. I'm also not sure the Mary Shelley-inspired premise was executed as well as it could have been, and I felt that way about most aspects of the film.

There are a couple of horror scenes I really enjoyed, specifically a solid curb-stomping sequence and a biting scene. After that, though, more didn't work than worked for me. I didn't love the fall, which relied a bit too heavily on CGI. The dance sequence could have been more fun, and the ending could have been more impactful.

In conclusion, The Bride tries to do something different. It will likely work for some viewers and not for others. I'd score it a 5/10.

raw_payload.author: e_k_cinephile
raw_payload.content:
This second directorial effort by Maggie Gyllenhaal reunites her with Jessie Buckley and focuses on Frankenstein's bride. You might expect a gothic horror/sci-fi film, but it instead chooses to be a crime/gangster/police/detective movie. The story is moved from early-1800s England to 1930s America. Its narrative, visual aesthetic, and cultural references are all shaped according to this new period and the Hollywood cinema of that era. It presents Frankenstein and his bride as a Bonnie-and-Clyde-style criminal couple.

It's an over-long and rather dull film. Its feminist subtexts feel amateurish and awkward. The only real strengths are the music and the cast: alongside Buckley and Christian Bale in the leading roles, Gyllenhaal also casts her husband and brother in the film, yet almost none of the supporting characters contribute meaningfully to the story. In other words, the talent of this strong ensemble feels wasted.

After this, I'd recommend re-watching the magnificent "Bride of Frankenstein" (1935) by James Whale!

raw_payload.author: reniruma
raw_payload.content:
The mary shelley intro immediately told me we were about to get weird weird. This is the joker musical multiverse chaos we were promised but with corsets. I knew within ten minutes this would either be genius or a disaster and somehow it's both

the vibes are intoxicating even when the script is fighting for its life. It looks expensive, dramatic, and slightly unhinged from frame one. This could've been so electric, but instead it just flirts with that electricity constantly and it kind of shocks itself and forgets what outlet it's plugged into

jessie buckley commits so hard she might've bruised. She plays chaos like it's couture. Christian bale simply showing up already improves the temperature of the room. His screen presence alone had me forgiving things i shouldn't. Together they have this strange, magnetic push-pull that almost carries the whole thing!

You can feel the director swinging for the fences every single scene. Ambition is oozing out of this thing but coherence is on vacation. It keeps stacking ideas like a jenga tower then acts shocked when it wobbles. The themes are right there staring at us, it wants to say something bold about autonomy and creation and identity, but they never sharpen into anything piercing. Chaotic doesn't automatically equal fearless, but i admire the attempt more than the result.

The empowerment messaging is loud but not always layered. It feels like it wants to scream women's power without digging into the why. There's a powerful story about refusing the role you were built for hiding in here somewhere! It's frustrating though because the ingredients for something radical are RIGHT THERE. Instead it just stays mildly unhinged, but visually fabulous

all of that aside, i cannot deny that this movie looks incredible. The craft is sincere, even when the story loses its grip. The production design understood the assignment AND extra credit. Cinematography? Lush, dramatic, dripping in gothic romance. Every frame feels handcrafted even when the plot is spiraling. It's the kind of movie you want to screenshot constantly

ultimately it's an entertaining, overstuffed, visually stunning near-masterpiece that almost becomes iconic but collapses under its own ambition which somehow makes it more fascinating than forgettable and honestly i'll probably still rewatch it just to stare at the madness again.

Letterboxd: reniruma !

raw_payload.author: fabiolpinheiro1993
raw_payload.content:
I'm all for weird, strange, unique and bonkers movies - but they have to work naturally and instantly pull you in. The Substance was a perfect example of that. The Bride, unfortunately, is the exact opposite.

It's always commendable when a director takes a big swing with their vision, but in this case it's a big swing and a big miss.

Maggie Gyllenhaal's take on The Bride of Frankenstein is technically superb. The costumes, visuals, cinematography, camera work and sound design are all astounding. It's clear she learned a lot from working with Christopher Nolan in that department, and in IMAX the film is visually striking.

But when it comes to the story, it's a mess of epic proportions.

There are simply too many things happening at once: too many characters, too many themes, too many ideas competing for attention. By the end, the film achieves nothing but a narrative overload that fails to deliver a satisfying resolution to any of the ideas it tries to explore. Everything feels like it's building toward something that never arrives.

On top of that, the film is full of logic issues and plot developments that make no sense, making the whole experience feel more pretentious than meaningful. Even worse, the story often contradicts the very messages it seems to be trying to convey, which suggests that while the director clearly had ambition and vision, the script never fully supported it.

The saving grace, beyond its technical achievements, are the performances. Jessie Buckley, Christian Bale and Annette Bening are all superb. Buckley in particular is magnetic, delivering a striking performance even in moments where the dialogue is nearly impossible to understand.

In the end, The Bride is nothing more than a failure of monstrous proportions.

raw_payload.author: TMAuthor23
raw_payload.content:
A massively misguided movie.

Hollywood swings and misses again.

Frankenstein (Hollywood not Mary Shelley's book) was originally conceived as a scare fest. A big hulking monster with bolts protruding from his neck, chasing people and being chased by villagers with pitchforks and torches. The "bride" in the first movie, and the book never lives. She is a total Hollywood invention, in the second Frankenstein movie.

At its core demographic, any Frankenstein property is supposed to appeal to horror movie fans. They should be scary.

This is not that.

Instead, we get Maggie Gyllenhall's attempt at a monster feminist. But the film isn't a comedy. There's dancing, noir-ish romance, and an uprising among women.

Sure. That's why people have shown up for Franky for decades. Uh...no.

And as much as new age silly scribes want to praise this, less than one tenth of one percent will ever see this based upon the anemic first week box office.

raw_payload.author: Sleepin_Dragon
raw_payload.content:
In 1930s Chicago, a lonely and isolated Frank seeks a companion and approaches the avant-garde Dr. Euphronius for assistance. Together, they successfully bring a murdered young woman named Ida back to life, intended to be Frank's perfect partner. However, once reanimated, the "Bride" develops a radical independence that neither of her creators anticipated...

Instead of the gothic horror promised by the marketing, we are treated to a confusing, aimless narrative that seems more interested in modern social commentary than actual substance. It attempts to blend the anarchy of Joker with the outlaw spirit of Bonnie and Clyde, but the result is a disorientating mess that leaves the audience wondering what the actual plot was supposed to be.

The script is, frankly, abysmal. It is a total vacuum of creativity that hides behind the term "reimagining" to mask its own lack of original ideas. Bringing Mary Shelley's name into this feels like a slight against a true genius; the writing here couldn't be brought back from the dead by any amount of lightning. It is lazy, pretentious, and incredibly thin, often veering into rants that feel entirely disconnected from the era or the characters.

Visually, the film is a bit of a mixed bag. While the dancing and music are messy and often intrusive, the makeup effects are actually quite impressive. There is a clear effort from the technical team to create something striking, but even the most polished exterior cannot save a hollow interior. It is a shame that such a high budget was seemingly funnelled into a project that ignores the basic necessity of a coherent screenplay.

The cast is the only reason this doesn't sink to a lower score, though they are utterly wasted here. Christian Bale and Jessie Buckley do their best with the material they are given, but they are trapped in a film that doesn't know how to use them. It is a genuinely robust disappointment, and judging by the number of people walking out of the theatre after thirty minutes, it's a flop in the making. A lazy, messy, and ultimately boring experience...

3/10.

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch

The story jumps between strange ideas involving aliens, mysterious portals, and possible spiritual elements, but it never commits to a clear direction. At times it almost feels like the film is about to make sense, yet it quickly drifts back into chaotic storytelling that struggles to find its footing.

Instead of building suspense or intrigue, the constant confusion makes the experience frustrating. A mystery driven film should guide viewers through its world while slowly revealing answers, but here the narrative often feels scattered and unfinished.

The performances also fail to elevate the material. The young actor at the center of the story delivers a performance that feels awkward and unpolished, making it hard to connect with the character. Overall, the film feels disorganized and ultimately becomes a frustrating watch.

It's gothic, it's tender, it's a little chaotic in places, but the emotion is so front and centre that I didn't even care when things got messy. The whole thing feels drenched in longing and obsession in the best way.

The acting is fantastic. You can feel every look, every hesitation, every desperate reach for connection. It reminded me of what 'Joker Folie à Deux' was trying to be emotionally - that heightened, romantic, slightly unhinged devotion - but this actually made me believe it. Not everything lands perfectly, but I don't think that's the point. It's not about neat storytelling. It's about the feeling.

And I did find this love story very believable. Twisted? Yes. Intense? Absolutely. But real in its vulnerability. It's one of those films that lingers because it dares to sit in the discomfort of love and identity. If you're into moody romance with substance and style, this one's going to hit.

raw_payload.author: ferguson-6
raw_payload.content:
Greetings again from the darkness. I became a Universal Monsters fan as soon as I reached the age where my parents would let me stay up to watch the later horror movies on Fridays and Saturdays. That love for the films and the stories and the monsters has only grown over the years, so you can only imagine the excitement I held for this film ... especially on the heels of last year's impressive FRANKENSTEIN from visionary Guillermo Del Toro. When my movie hopes and expectations reach this level, disappointment often follows; however, not this time!

You likely know Maggie Gylenhaal from her superb acting in such films as SECRETARY (2002), THE DARK KNIGHT ((2008), and CRAZY HEART (2009). You may not know that her feature film directorial debut was the excellent THE LOST DAUGHTER (2021), for which she also adapted the screenplay from the novel. The success of that 'little' film gave her the clout to create her dream project: a reimagining of James Whale's 1935 classic, BRIDE OF FRANKENSTEIN. For this one, Ms. Gyllenhaal directs her own incredibly creative and innovative tale, two adjectives that also describe her stunning and original script.

A big-time project requires a top-notch cast, and the filmmaker secured two of the best working today for her leads: Jessie Buckley and Oscar winner Christian Bale. The supporting cast is also impressive, and includes (5-time Oscar nominee) Annette Bening, Peter Sarsgaard (Maggie's real-life husband), Oscar winner Penelope Cruz, Jake Gylenhaal (Maggie's brother), and John Magaro (SEPTEMBER 5, 2024). The number of crossovers and intersections in the careers of these actors and Maggie are too many to list, but it speaks to the admiration these talented folks share for each other.

Obviously, Ms. Gyllenhaal's inspiration was borne from James Whale's two films, BRIDE OF FRANKENSTEIN (1935) and FRANKENSTEIN (1931), as well as the 1818 novel, "Frankenstein; or the Modern Prometheus", written by Mary Shelley. Certain touches within her new movie pay homage to all three, plus a bonus tip of the top hat to Mel Brooks' YOUNG FRANKENSTEIN (1974). Ms. Gyllenhaal opens the film in mob-filled 1930's Chicago with a truly fantastic prologue sequence that introduces us to Ida, the first of three characters played by Ms. Buckley. Her performance in this segment is nothing short of mesmerizing, and it's capped off by the ingenious inclusion of the deceased Mary Shelley ... a type of spin on the iconic opening scene of BRIDE OF FRANKENSTEIN. Soon after, we find 'Frank' (Mr. Bale) making his plea to Dr. Euphronius (Ms. Bening) and her intriguing assistant Greta (Jeannie Berlin) to reinvigorate a life partner that can cure his many years of loneliness.

As you would surmise, Dr. Euphronius is successful, and soon Frank and Penny (Ida's new name) have paired up and carve their way through society - often making headlines ala Bonnie and Clyde. Their road trips create moments of tenderness, longing, sensuality, and violence ... yes, plenty of violence. A radical women's social movement is started as a result of one of Penny's key feminist moments. Despite a blank memory, Penny understands the power of women. Movie musicals featuring hoofing star Ronnie Reed (Mr. Gylenhaal) play a crucial role in Frank's life, though it's not until later in the story that the reason is revealed. All this time, two detectives (Mr. Sarsgaard and Ms. Cruz) are tracking Frank and Penny, and we note the soft spot one of them holds for The Bride.

The film is a majestic triumph for Maggie Gylenhaal as a filmmaker, and surely will open many doors for future projects. Visually, the film is stunning to watch. The Bride and Frank are both magnificent creatures, yet every set and every scene are a feast for the eye and ears (yes, the sound is equally superb). The actors have all delivered their A-game, yet somehow Jessie Buckley continues to stand out (she's Oscar nominated this year for her memorable performance in HAMNET). It's not just her shock of white hair or stained face (explained in the film). No, the performance is much deeper and multi-faceted. Without spoiling anything, Ms. Buckley must adjust to the requirements of each scene, as well as the wide realm of emotions her roles demand. For fans, we do get the left-hand twitch, and as for viewing this film only once ... "I prefer not to." Opens in theaters March 6, 2026 (see it on the big screen!)

raw_payload.author: oNerdcall
raw_payload.content:
The Bride! Was born with ambition. And this is evident from the very first minutes. Maggie Gyllenhaal didn't just want to revisit a classic, she wanted to redefine it. The proposal stems from an old concern of the director's when she realized that, in Bride of Frankenstein, the character who gives the work its title has almost no voice. Here, the intention is clear: to give her that voice back. What makes the film interesting is also what, at times, causes it to stumble.

There is undeniable courage in this reinterpretation. The director is not afraid to tamper with something the audience already knows, nor to take a firm stance on identity, belonging, and choice. However, this firmness sometimes turns into insistence. The film wants so much to make its point clear that it ends up repeating, pointing out, and explaining what was already evident. Instead of strengthening the message, this diminishes its impact.

It is a film that gets more right than wrong, but not without stumbling blocks. Its ambition is admirable. Its proposal is relevant. The director shows personality and courage in tackling a classic and defending a clear point of view. What prevents the film from achieving something even greater is precisely the lack of balance between saying and showing, between trusting the image and trusting the word.

There is strength, there is originality, and there are memorable moments. When it stops trying to prove its point and simply embraces its own strangeness, the film shines. When it tries to be bigger than it already is, it ends up dragging on longer than necessary. Still, it's impossible to leave the theater indifferent. The Bride! May be imperfect, but its boldness and performances ensure that it will be remembered. And perhaps that is its greatest victory.

raw_payload.author: FilmoNerd
raw_payload.content:
I just watched this movie in IMAX during the opening week. There were only two people in the entire cinema, which was an accurate sign of things to come.

This movie is NEITHER an innovative retelling of a horror classic, NOR is a thrilling date movie for a gainfully employed couple.

This movie is just plain weird, targeted at the kind of people who keep reptiles as pets and think non-stop benders are a cool lifestyle.

Jessie Buckley's over-the-top performance would shine in a neighborhood artsy theater production, but it does not work in a cinema. Studio bosses were correct to doubt this casting choice, before ultimately giving in to Maggie Gyllenhaal's demands.

A gifted actor like Christian Bale is handicapped by the off-beat energy from his co-star, and choices made by the script and director.

POTENTIAL SPOILERS BELOW: =========================

I try to avoid spoilers, but there are two themes that I must talk about in this review:

The movie unnecessarily portrays all men as evil, where the bride is getting assaulted by men at the club, or groped by men in uniform, or sa'ed by a police detective, despite being disheveled psychotic and puking out black liquid.

The anti-men message continues when Police sergeants in three different jurisdictions keep ignoring one detective solely because she's female. Literally every male character is shown in negative light, so much so that I felt like screaming "come onnnnn" in ridicule.

This could have been a brilliant movie with edgy, artsy retelling of a horror story that would thrill a couple watching it. But script writer + director Maggie Gyllenhaal completely manages to destroy her chance through pretentious artsiness, forced anti-men messages, and poor choice of a leading lady.

HARD PASS.

raw_payload.author: jp_91
raw_payload.content: Throughout film history, Mary Shelley's novel "Frankenstein" has been adapted into numerous films and versions of all kinds. In 2026, "The Bride!" was released, a loose adaptation that falls into the subgenre of gothic romance and dark humor. Directed by Maggie Gyllenhaal, who also does a tremendous job, the film adds elements reminiscent of Bonnie and Clyde and even Romeo and Juliet to the famous Frankenstein monster story, along with a powerful social critique of misogyny and aperspective on gender equality. The cast delivers masterful performances. Jessie Buckley portrays a magnificent character full of dramatic nuances, and Christian Bale is brilliant as Frank, at times delivering a performance reminiscent of Herman Munster. Both he and Buckley have fantastic on-screen chemistry. Annette Bening is tremendous and ferocious in her role, Jeannie Berlin delivers a hilarious performance in every scene she's in, Peter Sarsgaard gives a powerful and even sentimental performance, Penélope Cruz portrays a charming, tender, and funny character, while Jake Gyllenhaal navigates between amusing and unlikeable. The cinematography is superb, achieving stunning visuals. The costume design is iconic. The makeup effects are realistic and high-quality. "The Bride!" is a film that breathes new life into Mary Shelley's classic story with a unique twist.

The Bride is definitely chaotic by design but nothing about it feels controlled in the slightest. It's basically the cinematic equivalent of throwing everything at the wall and seeing what sticks and very little does. It tries to do and say so many things that it essentially ends up lacking any clearly defined identity outside of it's more outlandish elements. Because for as much as it has only display I felt nothing except for mind numbing boredom.

There's precious few things that kept me engaged and even the things I like don't bring it close to making this a good movie for me. For as all over the place as this movie is Jessie Buckley's performance plays every chaotic and inconsistent beat perfectly. It's a scenery chewing performance for sure but she's always so energetic and captivating on screen that I was always at the very least entertained by the work she was doing. It's the sort of fearless acting I always really admire and she's proving herself to be a true chameleon with each performance she gives. It's also vibrant in a very colourful way so it's always at least nice to look at as an overall production but honestly nothing more. For as admittedly unique as this movie is it just has now idea of what's it's trying to say or have a consistent idea of how to execute it.

The film feels like it's dipping its toe into a new genre and tone with every other scene and the whole thing just buckles under the pressure of how much it's trying to balance making the whole experience an overwhelmingly dull one. It's part Bonnie and Clyde, part police procedural, part social upheaval and a half-hearted commentary on societal sexism and none of it works. After the film changes its identity for the second or third time I just stopped caring when I knew it had no actual plot to latch onto with a framing device I absolutely hated which the film just seems to forget about at a certain point. It doesn't feel like there's anything of an actual story so the film just stumbles from one scene to the next without giving any real sense of a rising action. Even Christian Bale as Frankenstein's Monster feels wasted as he's constantly playing second fiddle to The Bride and just along for the journey without any agency of his own after the first act.

Part of me wants to admire The Bride! For being as audacious as it is but admiration certainly doesn't translate to enjoyment because this was the most unsatisfying experience I've had watching a movie in a while. For everything it throws at you there's just nothing to actually care about and I don't have the slightest idea of what it was trying to say or accomplish when all was said and done. I'm not sad that this movie exists but I just couldn't tell you why it does.

raw_payload.author: MorfenZ
raw_payload.content: I had high hopes for The Bride! By Maggie Gyllenhaal, but unfortunately it didn't fulfill them. As expected, Christian Bale and Jessie Buckley both deliver strong performances, and the film tries to mix comedy, mystery, and drama into one story. There are moments where the direction gives off some great old-school vibes, which I appreciated. Still, the overall concept and execution didn't fully click for me, and I struggled to connect with what the film was trying to do. Despite an interesting idea, it never really pulled me in i definitely expected much more.

raw_payload.author: Thatmovieguy144

raw_payload.author: LiamWatchesFilms
raw_payload.content:
When I was in school I had to make a short movie. Only a few minutes in length. I decided that I had such a genius idea that I didn't want to work in a group, I was doing it alone. After several weeks, I submitted a movie that was nothing more than pictures of tanks flashing on screen to the sound of a System of a Down song. I was so sure that this idea was genius. I mean, I had pictures of tanks and a song about war. How deep and thoughtful I was. Surely everyone would see how complex my idea was.

I was so sure of the greatness of my movie that when I played it to the class, I was oblivious to the embarrassment everyone else was experiencing on my behalf. Now I'm older, smarter and a little wiser, I can look back at the abysmal film I made with the appropriate level of shame.

As I sat watching this film, I was reminded of that time. This was a film with some basic ideas that the director seems to believe are ground breaking, deep and revolutionary.

She's thrown together a series of half formulated ideas without really understanding how they fit together.

That's not to say the ideas are bad. They've just not been developed properly, just like my own war movie. I neither liked nor cared about any of the characters, and that's a huge issue. By the half way point I just wanted it to end. Honestly, this rambling review is far more coherent than the movie.

raw_payload.author: jordansmith-92432

raw_payload.author: RidayB

Christian Bale is fantastic, adds emotional depth to the monster; a characterisation that may feel banal over the years, however, Bale is exciting throughout. The Bride! Jessie Buckley is one heck of an actress, high on the madness, loud and fierce in her expression. She manages controlled but chaotic bursts of emotions. These two, forward the momentum and leave an intense and tender experience in a screenplay that can feel gimmicky in places.

raw_payload.author: RuthW-840

raw_payload.author: MariaV-970

raw_payload.author: Jithindurden

raw_payload.author: a_vander
raw_payload.content:
"The Bride!" sets out on a road adventure with Frankenstein (Christian Bale) -a name borrowed from his creator- and Ida (Jessie Buckley), a young woman resurrected in the same manner as him. Together, they share a fondness for cinema. Set in the late 1920s and early 1930s, the pair frequent crowded theaters and even manage to slip into a glamorous party alongside the stars of the era. Yet their escapades soon turn violent, drawing the attention of the police.

The production design is clever and solid, and the score is capably performed. Unfortunately, that's where the praise ends. The story itself is little more than a "hit and run," lacking substance or depth. I do admire the meticulous effort poured into the atmosphere -those primitive vehicles restored to perfection, the period details carefully arranged. The musicians also deserve recognition. But let's be honest: the backbone of any film is its narrative, and here it's so thin it could fit on a brightly colored sticky note, barely two square inches wide.

No amount of talent, in front of or behind the camera, can salvage a film when the script collapses under its own weight. When the Universal Pictures logo appeared at the start, I was reminded of their nickname, "The House of Horrors," earned during their early mastery of the genre. Watching "The Bride!" only reinforced the feeling that Universal desperately needs to hire -or rehire, if they let it go away- serious talent in the art of storytelling.

ADDENDUM - I must clarify: "Warner Bros desperately needs serious talent in the art of storytelling". It turns out that this movie is entirely produced and distributed by Warner, but in a cross-studio arrangement, Universal appears as the owner of the original movie made in 1935, thus, they hold the rights over the intellectual property and their logo appears as part of this deal.

raw_payload.author: dromasca
raw_payload.content:
Maggie Gyllenhaal seems to me to be one of the success stories among actors who have gone behind the camera and became directors. After the remarkable debut with 'The Lost Daughter' now comes 'The Bride!' which is more than a confirmation of a complete mastery of the tools of the trade. It is a completely different film in terms of theme, style and visual experience, belonging to a genre (of horror films inspired by Mary Shelley's novel) that already has dozens of titles in a continuously growing list and in which highly experienced directors such as Guillermo del Torro have only launched themselves after many years of preparation. There is only one common thread between 'The Bride!' and Maggie Gyllenhaal's first film - that of a declared feminism devoid of complexes and clichés, that kind of militancy whose artistic quality also makes it effective in promoting messages.

The monster created by Dr. Frankenstein in 1819 appears after more than a century of suffering and loneliness in 1936 Chicago, where an eccentric scientist experiments with bringing dead organisms back to life. Her wish to find a soul mate (or, if you will, a monster mate) is fulfilled, but the one brought back to life is a woman murdered by the local mob and, on top of that, infiltrated by the ghost of none other than Mary Shelley. A relationship will develop between the two that evolves into a love story as incredible as it is inevitable. As if in a film like 'Bonnie and Clyde' the two set off on the roads of 1930s America, hunted by the whole world: by the police, by gangsters, by hysterical crowds who would like to repeat the lynching attempt in the original film. Has the story of Romeo and Juliet found its equivalent? If death is not final, perhaps even the decisions of fate can be contested.

Maggie Gyllenhaal - who also wrote the screenplay for this new variation on the fate of Mary Shelley's characters - has created a cinematic and visual universe in which the writer's world, 1930s gangster films, the sin cities of Batman and Joker films and contemporary feminist themes meet. 1930s pop culture also receives a deep reverence: the monster is a fan of musical romantic comedies with an actor (played by brother Jake Gyllenhaal). Several musical numbers pace the story, all well done, although they seem more like contemporary disco numbers than scenes with music and dance from the last century. Maggie Gyllenhaal is an actress turned director who works wonderfully with actors. Jessie Buckley plays the lead role or roles and with this film she reinforces my belief that she is one of the best actresses of her generation. Christian Bale starts from replicating the physiognomy of Boris Karloff to create a monster who becomes the most human character of the film. Annette Bening, Penélope Cruz and Peter Sarsgaard are a pleasure to watch, even if their roles are not the most consistent of their respective careers. For me 'The Bride!' was a successful cinematic experience, exactly the quality entertainment I needed these days.

raw_payload.author: hvdcth

raw_payload.author: barnabaponchielli
raw_payload.content: Finally, a Rob Zombie film worthy of the best White Zombie's music, the limp and grotesque romanticism of Baz Luhrman's Moulin Rouge realized, a gothic, female-led Natural Born Killers, a #metoo Joker, a feminist Sin City: Buckley surpasses Emma Stone, Bale is on par with DiCaprio in One Battle, and Gyllenhaal is splendidly insane for directing and writing such a wonderfully crafted madness, a tribute to Mary Shelley's ghost and capable of renewing Tarantino's pulp aesthetic and ethos! This is 100% theater and literature on film. I was left speechless for the entire two-hour running time, in total suspension of disbelief, explosive and hilarious beyond measure.

raw_payload.author: marajade-22265

"Here Comes The Razzie Award!"

I have no idea what this movie was trying to achieve besides the title of worst-ever movie in Hollywood history! The Bride just overtook my #1 Worst-Ever Movie spot from Battlefield Earth. I never thought there would come a day that that would happen.

The absurd feminism in this reimagining (Hollywood, STOP reimagining! No one wants you to do it!) of such classic literature fails miserably! I can't imagine being so desperate for attention as to write this garbage and think it's incredible. Who at Warner Bros. Thought this was a good enough idea to waste $85 million dollars on?

The only thing good I can say is that the actors and actresses looked like they had fun filming it. But still, I have no idea why any of them walked down the aisle thinking it was a marriage made in heaven. Maybe the ridiculousness of it all had them engaged for some odd reason. raw_payload.author: vengeance20
raw_payload.content:
So, I'd only heard about this one & seen some previews which looked alright, but still, this not being quite my sort of film, I wasn't bought. I then went to watch it because it sort of came under the Horror Genre.

In 1936 Chicago, Frankenstein asks Dr. Euphronius to help create a companion. They give life to a murdered woman as the Bride, sparking romance, police interest, & radical social change.

I found the film to be not bad & weirdly enjoyable. The story-line is simple & while not the greatest, was a bit of fresh air given these types of films have convoluted & confusing plots that make no sense. But here, the plot was more to the point.

The cast do well with their parts & play their roles well. The film has plenty of eye candy with the women & some gory action & violence here. The films gothic look was well done & there were some dark themes going on here too. I will say I liked the setting of the 1930's.

The films runtime is long at 1 hour & 57 minutes (117 minutes) & has not bad pacing. I will say the film sort of drags near the end & could've benefitted from some cutting & editing here. The ending itself was as good as could be & ended well enough.

Overall, it's good film & one that I will say I surprisingly enjoyed. As I said, it's not amazing, but it's pretty good & one I'd say you should watch at least once. Give this one a watch.

7/10.

raw_payload.author: Stcyyy
raw_payload.content:
This film just felt uncomfortable the whole time. There are aspects about it that are not revealed in the movie previews, which turned the story into something completely different than what I thought it would be. If you are not a fan of experiencing secondhand embarrassment in a film, this one is not for you. I could barely get through the introductory scene and I almost walked out of the theater less than halfway through the movie (however, there were people in the audience laughing and enjoying the film, so maybe you will have a different opinion). While there are some aspects of the story about feminism and consent that I can respect, it felt a bit strange in how those details were brought into the story. The acting was good for the script that the actors and actresses were given, it was just an odd story I didn't prefer. It also felt like a part of the story was missing, particularly Ida's past that led up to the opening scene. I think showing her past would have added to the story. There's one aspect of the story that felt unnecessary about Ida and another mystery character but I won't give that away. I don't know how to explain this but it just felt gross the whole time in that you are constantly staring at the jagged scars of Frankenstein's monster, the stained skin and injuries of Ida/Penny, the dirty streets, gross jars of body parts from the mafia, and icky sleeping quarters. Some scenes were just overstimulating with flashing lights, jerking movements, and loud music and yelling (I can handle loud movies and fast action sequences, but this one was just different). Overall, this movie wasn't for me.

Pros: while I didn't like this movie, the costume design and makeup was good, the acting was good despite the story line, the sets and props did seem to be accurate to the period, and the story did try to get across some messages to the audience about society and women. This was a brave script to attempt. If you watch this movie, go in without expectations of what the story will be about.

raw_payload.author: matepolcz
raw_payload.content:
The Bride! Is gorgeous, but other than that it's hard to watch, for me at least.

I think this story could be something I'd enjoy watching, but this approach is something I dislike. They wanted to do too much and ended up not delivering.

The cinematography is gorgeous, one of the most beautiful digitally shot films of the past few years. The editing is good too with the wonderful dance sequences and even though I didn't care for the story, it wasn't too boring. The sound design and score are mixed, because there were times, when I really liked them. The cast is amazing and the performances are good too, but since I didn't like the characters, I couldn't enjoy the performances too much.

The Bride! Is not for me and hopefully my biggest disappointment of the year.

raw_payload.author: garygwilliams
raw_payload.content: Actually, many scenes took me back to various classic films. The problem was that most of the film seemed like a patchwork of stolen memories closer to plagiarism than respectful tribute. Jesse Buckley was tremendous and Christian Bale was eh. The myriad of stars added little value which seemed a waste. Busby Berkeley arise!

raw_payload.author: steiner-sam
raw_payload.content:
It's a MeToo Frankenstein comedy drama set in 1936 in Chicago, New York City, and places in between. Mary Shelly (Jessie Buckley), author of the 1818 "Frankenstein," tells us of another story she always wanted to tell. Ida (Jessie Buckley), an apparent flapper type, draws the ire of gangster Lupino (Zlatko Buric) and is killed. Meanwhile, Frank (Christian Bale) arrives at Dr. Cornelia Euphronious's (Annette Bening) home, asking for a bride, since he has never had a close relationship with a woman. They dig up Ida and return her to life. The movie then follows the relationship of Ida and Frank, Frank's passion for movies, especially musicals featuring Ronnie Reed (Jake Gyllenhaal), and Ida's strongly feminist perspectives from her former life. They get into trouble, requiring the pursuit of Police Detective Jake Wiles (Peter Sarsgaard) and his assistant, Myrna (Penelope Cruz).

"The Bride" is cleverly written and performed, but confusing in spots because of rapid pacing and barely intelligible dialogue. The opening scene leading to Ida's death is confusing because of this. "The Bride" contains lots of tongue-in-cheek humor as well as violence. Frank's and Ida's passion for 1930s movies is delightful. The film is a well-expressed feminist statement. Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale work very well together, but a slower pace would have allowed them more creative space. I'll want to watch "The Bride" again at some future time when I can have subtitles.

raw_payload.author: kosmasp
raw_payload.content:
Well the movie even uses a curse word - but you know what is coming (or who is coming?) ... yes it is the Bride and yes no pun intended. Jessie Buckley again cements her place in actors heaven/pantheon ... or whatever you want to call it.

Everyone is great of course - even Jake Gyllenhall (the directors brother), who has a smaller but still a very significant role. Now you may know the Frankenstein story - but where would he end up ... if he lived alone for so many years? Life can get and be lonely ... it is not about highs ...quick highs that is, but about something more substantial.

That said, is society ready for this? Or rather was it ready? Because while it plays in the "future", it still is the past from our time. This looks amazing and just the central performance from the Bride alone is enough to recommend this. This being a Frankenstein story ... remember it can get violent and brutal! One scene is able to rival one very infamous scene from American History X .. you have been warned.

There is additional scene during the credits ... and you get a short audio after the credits - in case you stuck around, don't leave too early without seeing (and hearing?) it all.

The title may already suggest it, but this is quite female driven - some may even call it feminist in a sense. Now hopefully that does not have a negative connotation with you - it shouldn't ... and the movie is really well done, no matter what you think of certain aspects.

raw_payload.author: bornthespian-08995

raw_payload.author: richardlmills
raw_payload.content: The previews for this movie were fantastic. I couldn't wait to go. I even went to a 1:00 p.m. Matinee to see it. It was terrible. It was disjointed and had very little plot . The character development was the worst I've ever seen. I loved Jessie Buckley in Hamnet but this was pure garbage I'm afraid that Christian Bale may have ended his career with this performance. The only redeeming attribute was the choice of music in two places. That smile it put on my face was not worth 2 hours of agony. Please find something else more fun to do, like extensive dental work, rather than to waste your time at this movie.

raw_payload.author: HabibieHakim123
raw_payload.content:
It's basically Mary Shelley's version of Bonnie and Clyde, a chaotic and bloody relationship at the center of the story, the Bride, directed by Maggie Gyllenhaal, feels like a messy film to me, some of the chaos parts are well-structured, while others lean into chaos in a way that never really lands with much impact.

The film starts by focusing on the creation of the Bride and the Monster, played by Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale, Buckley had the potential to deliver another outstanding performance after her acclaimed work in Hamnet, and in many ways she does give a strong performance here, but it also feels somewhat held back by the film itself, with stronger writing and clearer direction, her performance could have been truly incredible.

Bale's portrayal of the Monster didn't fully convince me at first, early on it feels a bit restrained, but once the story descends into chaos, his performance starts to make much more sense, that's where Bale really finds the character, and you begin to see the wild commitment he brings to the role, of course, he's a great actor, but the film takes its time before letting him fully unleash that energy.

The two leads are solid overall, and most of the supporting cast does a decent job, however, Penélope Cruz unfortunately gives what i thought was the weakest performance in the film, i wouldn"t even say it's a good performance, which is disappointing considering how much a single weak performance can weigh down parts of a movie.

In the end, The Bride is a strange experience, i really appreciate some of the storytelling and stylistic choices that Gyllenhaal brings to the film, when the chaos works, it's genuinely exciting to watch, but there are also moments where the chaos feels flat and doesn't achieve the impact it seems to aim for, which is frustrating because it could have been really great.

Was i disappointed? A little bit, do i regret watching it? Not at all, despite its flaws, it's still a worthwhile and unusual experience, the film feels like a project full of great ideas and potential that never fully comes together, it's a mix of things that work and things that don't, resulting in a chaotic and messy film that remains, in its own way, quite interesting.

raw_payload.author: doug-810-515110
raw_payload.content:
What could have been a good - even great - idea is quickly squandered as the director puts her characters through a series of disjointed events. There is little time for discovery once the story gets rolling. The whole conceit that Mary Shelly inhabits the soul of one poor woman who is killed and then reanimated starts fine, but soon becomes an annoying device. Once Ida/Penny and Frank are let loose, the film becomes a crash course, careening from one set piece to another. The cinematography is great, as well as the production design, but the whole thing looks like it was edited in a blender. There's some nifty potential here, but is often details. The two fugitives wind up first at a nightclub - and get a taste of freedom. Howevwr, you get no real flow-through because the director hacks it up with strobe-like editing. It could have been exhilarating, but instead becomes perplexing and nearly incomprehensible. A dance number in a club holds promise as an important, elegiac revelation soon becomes annoying- over-staged and over-stated. You find yourself asking: who are these character and why are they here? Screenwriting 101 could have helped create a more cogent story. But obviously the director isn't interested in that. She'd rather create a retro MTV video rant about feminine with the subtlety of an anvil dropping from the sky. Anachronisms abound. Character revelations are spooled out just in time to fill in sorely missing development. By the time the characters start yelling "me too!" you'll be ready to admit defeat.

The actors are blameless in all of those. They do as best they can under the circumstances.

raw_payload.author: SpyroDungeon
raw_payload.content: What we have here is a truly gothic romantic drama. The film opens with dark comedy elements at one point, then veers into Dadaist and slightly surreal themes. With its entertaining scenes and slightly violent atmosphere, I really enjoyed it. The effort to experiment with different things is certainly evident. Jessie Buckley does a really good job, and she and Christian Bale, as Frankenstein, form a very complex duo. The film also draws you in with its references to contemporary elements, and its highly media-driven theme demonstrates this. Penélope Cruz's detective-like character was a cornerstone, in my opinion. I really liked this different style and found it quite entertaining. I really appreciate seeing different kinds of films in cinema, especially if you, like me, enjoy romantic crime films in the style of Bonnie and Clyde.

raw_payload.author: housermichael
raw_payload.content:
I am a fan of Jesse Buckley as an actress from her work on the film "Women Talking". But I am not a fan of the movie "Hamnet" for which she is nominated for the best actress Oscar this year. I thought it was a snoozefest and a terrible movie.

But now she stars in the new movie "The Bride!", written and directed and co-produced by Maggie Gyllenhaal. While I have not seen Maggie's previous writing and directing effort called "The Lost Daughter" from 2021, based on this new movie "The Bride!" she is a great filmmaker.

I loved this movie. Christian Bale gives another excellent performance as Frankenstein's monster who wants a scientist played by Annette Bening to create a female companion for him. So they dig up the dead Jesse Buckley character and bring her back to life. Then the two of them go on a crime spree across the country like "Bonnie and Clyde" or "Thelma and Louise". They kill a number of people. The film takes place in America in the 1930's.

Jake Gyllenhaal plays a movie actor that Christian's character admires. And Peter Sarsgaard and Penelope Cruz play detectives trying to catch the two criminals on the run.

All the actors in this film give superb performances. The production design by Karen Murphy who worked on "Elvis", is very good. The costume design by Sandy Powell who worked on "Shakespeare in Love", "The Aviator", and "The Young Victoria" is also very good.

The only thing I didn't like about the movie was the cinematography by Lawrence Sher. The film is frequently way too dark and sometimes you can't even see the actor's faces.

But this is a minor drawback. The writing, directing and acting are all brilliant. The use of music is very good. This is the best movie of 2026 so far. Very entertaining and very highly recommended. Go see it!

raw_payload.author: artrobles1964
raw_payload.content: Painful. Right from the start with the Mary Shelley bit. Nightclub scene didn't start well then was miserable to sit through during possession soliloquy. I could have walked out right but for it being date night. I seriously covered my eyes and groaned when Frank did his dance number. Did she really find inspiration in Young Frankenstein? Sooo stupid. Will never watch any of her subsequent work.

raw_payload.author: JeffreyRHurt
raw_payload.content:
I like an artistic movie as much as the next person, but I fail to grasp the art in this movie. Is it a musical? A horror movie? A romance? An homage to Bonnie & Clyde? The writing fails to create a decent narrative.

The beginning story honestly doesn't seem to matter other than "producing" The Bride. The mafia underground/police corruption storyline could have been enough, but that wasn't even kept. The romance part was decent, but kept getting interrupted by useless story threads. The concept of them being monsters, also ruined because of musical scenes.

How many writers were on this movie? If it was only one, I question whether they had a fever or suffer from ADHD. It could have been so many things and ended up being worse than leftovers at the end of the week.
